title: ANZ urges back to future for banks
full_text:
Australian banks should re-enter wealth management and insurance to improve their profitability, says ANZ chief executive Nuno Matos, areas they abandoned in the aftermath of the damaging banking royal commission.
Speaking at investment bank Morgan Stanley's annual conference, Matos highlighted a steep decline in the big four's return on capital over the past decade, which he linked to the narrower suite of services they now offer.
"Many, many businesses went out of the banking industry, like wealth management, insurance, just to name some of it ... and what we have seen as a result of that is an industry which is highly impacted [by margins]," Matos said.
"We have to find a way to go back into some parts of customer needs, like wealth management, insurance, that we stopped doing."
He cited the banking sector's declining profitability, particularly declining rates of return on capital which fell from 16 to 17 per cent 15 years ago to about 9 to 10 per cent now.
The trend was the opposite of what global peers had experienced over the same period, he said, adding that Australia had all the ingredients to grow: education, resources, energy and proximity to Asia, but had "failed to capitalise" on its advantages. "In the last 20 years, for some reason, we were not able to materialise that into growth," he said. "In two decades Australia grew less than 1 per cent [in per capita terms].
"The country, in my opinion, needs to reflect on that ... We have been deciding on the safe side, and there is a cost for that."
In the aftermath of the 2017 Hayne royal commission, Commonwealth Bank, Westpac, National Australia Bank and ANZ all announced, to varying extents, their withdrawal from providing financial advice, investment management and related services, pointing to rising regulatory costs and dwindling profit margins.
Matos, who has just completed his first year leading the big four lender, said Labor's federal budget was aligned with the government's broader policy goals. He declined to comment on the merits of changes to the capital gains tax regime, which have drawn criticism from business leaders including Commonwealth Bank chief executive Matt Comyn.
He added the intent of changes to the CGT and negative gearing in the housing market were to improve affordability, shift ownership away from landlords, encourage more home building and incentivise new-home buyers, adding that "the policy that was put in place clearly pushes into that direction". But he conceded the measures would slow mortgage market growth and investor lending would also go down.
"We have no doubt the mortgage market will slow down. It has been growing at around 8 to 9 per cent. It will probably go to 5 to 6 per cent in the short- to medium-term.
"From a price perspective, who knows? Clearly, there will be less price appreciation," Matos said, while declining to speculate on whether prices could actually fall.
Matos also revealed ANZ was preparing to start an in-house academy to train more frontline business bankers. Since his arrival as chief executive, Matos controversially announced ANZ would cut 3500 jobs and lose another 1000 contractors.
"We want to train new ones [bankers], the ones we have today, it is about having better equipped bankers," he said.
The chief executive was hesitant to frame artificial intelligence as a way to axe head count, arguing instead that he wants to use AI to increase the nation's living standards.
ANZ shares gained 0.8 per cent to $34.56 on Wednesday, and are down almost 5 per cent so far this year in line with broader declines across the big banks.
Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited
author: Angira Bharadwaj and Jonathan Shapi
heading: Banking and Economy
